logistics.drive_notes: I-70 westbound. Traction laws frequently active. Severe weekend traffic bottlenecks at the Eisenhower Tunnel.
logistics.lodging_notes: Silverthorne, Frisco, and Dillon serve as central budget hubs. On-mountain lodging is premium.

metadata.reputation: Denver's local mountain. Famous for endless moguls and great tree skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible glades (Mary Jane), heavily bumped out main runs, steep alpine chutes in the Cirque.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Consistently good cold snow. Winds can scour the top but fill in the trees nicely.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high on weekends, but the Mary Jane side spreads experts out well.
metadata.logistics_notes: The Winter Park Express train runs directly from Denver Union Station right to the lifts.

metadata.reputation: High-alpine soul mountain with the longest season in Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Legendary steep hike-to chutes (East Wall) and intense high-alpine bowls. Very little beginner terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely high base elevation guarantees cold, dry snow when lower resorts get heavy snow or rain.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low mid-week, moderate on weekends. Highly favored by locals.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires driving over Loveland Pass (US-6) which frequently closes during storms.

metadata.reputation: No-frills, old-school local favorite situated directly on the Continental Divide.
metadata.terrain_character: Wide open, above-treeline bowls. Lift 9 accesses incredible high-alpine ridges.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive wind-buff. Stays incredibly cold.
metadata.crowd_factor: Generally low. Avoids the worst I-70 crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located *before* the Eisenhower tunnel, saving 30+ minutes of traffic compared to Summit County.

metadata.reputation: Perfectly naturally-separated terrain from beginner to expert.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible high alpine bowls (Tucker Mountain) and great sustained pitch.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Classic Colorado dry powder. High elevation keeps it well preserved.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on weekends, but the natural separation prevents extreme bottlenecks.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free parking available in Alpine lot with shuttles.

metadata.reputation: Massive resort attached to a historic mining town. Incredible high alpine.
metadata.terrain_character: High alpine bowls (Imperial Express) are amazing, but the lower mountain acts as a flat bottleneck.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High elevation holds snow perfectly, but high winds above treeline can severely scour the bowls.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely crowded. The lower mountain base areas get heavily congested.
metadata.logistics_notes: Parking in town is expensive; relies heavily on gondola access from the main lots.

metadata.reputation: Family-heavy frontside with a massive, rugged footprint in the back.
metadata.terrain_character: Dercum Mountain is all cruisers, but The Outback and North Peak offer incredibly steep, tight tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: The Outback holds powder for days because the frontside crowds rarely venture back that far.
metadata.crowd_factor: Frontside is a zoo; the Outback hides the crowds perfectly.
metadata.logistics_notes: Offers some of the best night skiing in Colorado.

metadata.reputation: Sprawling luxury mega-resort with world-famous Back Bowls.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless open bowl skiing, deep glades in Blue Sky Basin, and massive front-side groomers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great snow, but the sheer volume of skiers tracks out the main runs quickly.
metadata.crowd_factor: Front-side bottlenecks are brutal, but advanced riders can easily escape crowds in the back bowls.
metadata.logistics_notes: Extremely expensive parking. Best to carpool or use the bus from outer lots.

metadata.reputation: Luxury grooming and free cookies, often overlooked by powder chasers.
metadata.terrain_character: Impeccable groomers, but Grouse Mountain hides incredibly steep, demanding tree runs and bump lines.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Slightly lower elevation means the base can get warm late season, but the upper trees hold snow well.
metadata.crowd_factor: Consistently less crowded than Vail or Breckenridge on a powder day.
metadata.logistics_notes: Further down I-70, adding 20+ mins to the drive from Denver.

logistics.drive_notes: Long drive past Glenwood Canyon. Highway 82 can be treacherous.
logistics.lodging_notes: Aspen/Snowmass proper is luxury pricing. Budget lodging requires a 40+ minute commute from down-valley (Basalt, Carbondale, or Glenwood Springs).

metadata.reputation: The sprawling giant of the Aspen area. Something for everyone.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive vertical, incredible hike-to alpine terrain (Hanging Valley), and endless cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Consistently excellent cold, dry powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Its massive size absorbs crowds effortlessly.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free shuttle buses connect all four Aspen mountains.

metadata.reputation: Classic steep skiing dumping right into the heart of downtown Aspen.
metadata.terrain_character: No green runs. Steep sustained pitches, tight trees, and heavy bumps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Holds snow well on the upper mountain, but the face can get baked in the afternoon.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low to moderate on powder days. Most people favor Snowmass for sheer acreage.
metadata.logistics_notes: You can walk to the gondola from downtown Aspen.

metadata.reputation: The crown jewel of Colorado steep skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: Highlands Bowl is a legendary 45-minute hike-to powder experience. Insanely steep.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Highlands Bowl preserves powder for days after a storm due to its aspect.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. The physical effort required keeps the crowds away.
metadata.logistics_notes: Not a mountain for beginners or casual cruising.

metadata.reputation: Home of the X-Games, but primarily known as a beginner mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: Very flat, rolling groomers and massive terrain parks. Tiehack side has some decent pitched cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower elevation means snow quality degrades faster than Highlands or Snowmass.
metadata.crowd_factor: Virtually empty on powder days since all the experts go to Highlands or Aspen Mountain.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great option if you want untouched corduroy or massive terrain parks.

metadata.reputation: A hidden, low-cost local gem just outside Glenwood Springs.
metadata.terrain_character: One of the steepest sustained pitches in the state (The Heathen). Great, tight tree runs.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: A bit lower in elevation, but heavily protected by trees.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Mostly locals from Glenwood Springs.
metadata.logistics_notes: Offers a much cheaper alternative to the Aspen mountains while still catching similar storm tracks.

logistics.drive_notes: Rabbit Ears Pass can be highly dangerous during a storm. Bypasses the worst of I-70.
logistics.lodging_notes: Plenty of lodging in Steamboat Springs, ranging from budget to luxury.

metadata.reputation: The 'Champagne Powder' capital of Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Arguably the best perfectly-spaced tree skiing in the state (Shadows and Closets). Very few steep chutes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unique northern storm cells that miss I-70 completely. Snow is incredibly light.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on powder days, but massive acreage spreads it out.
metadata.logistics_notes: Base elevation is very low for Colorado (6,900ft), making the very bottom susceptible to warming.

logistics.drive_notes: Extreme high-friction drive from Denver. Requires a multi-day trip commitment.
logistics.lodging_notes: Telluride is wildly expensive. Purgatory/Durango offers much more affordable options.

metadata.reputation: The most beautiful mountain town in America with jaw-dropping extreme terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible steep chutes, massive hike-to terrain (Palmyra Peak), and long, gorgeous groomers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches southern storms. High elevation preserves the powder perfectly.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Its extreme isolation prevents casual weekend crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free gondola connects the town to the mountain village.

metadata.reputation: Laid back, family-friendly resort near Durango.
metadata.terrain_character: Cruisy, undulating terrain. Great, widely-spaced trees. Lacks extreme steeps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Needs a good southern storm track, but holds snow well.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Walk-on lifts are common even on powder days.
metadata.logistics_notes: 30-minute drive north of Durango.

metadata.reputation: Hardcore, experts-only freeride mecca.
metadata.terrain_character: Un-groomed, intense avalanche terrain. Only one lift; heavily reliant on hiking or heli-drops.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely high elevation. Deep, untouched powder.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires avalanche gear (beacon, shovel, probe). Guided-only for most of the primary powder season.

logistics.drive_notes: Wolf Creek Pass is notoriously treacherous during snowstorms.
logistics.lodging_notes: No on-mountain lodging. Stay in Pagosa Springs (30 mins west) or South Fork (30 mins east).

metadata.reputation: The undisputed snow magnet of Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Consistently gets the most snow in the state. Short but fun, steep pitches and hike-to ridges.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High base elevation guarantees deep, cold powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Remote location keeps lift lines to a minimum.
metadata.logistics_notes: Incredibly affordable lift tickets compared to mega-resorts.

logistics.drive_notes: Route 50 over Monarch pass can be difficult, but typically avoids I-70 gridlock.
logistics.lodging_notes: Gunnison and Salida offer cheap lodging. Mt. Crested Butte is more premium.

metadata.reputation: Extreme, rocky steeps and a fiercely independent town culture.
metadata.terrain_character: The Extremes (North Face, Headwall) are some of the steepest lift-accessed terrain in North America.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredibly rocky. Needs a massive base to safely open the best terrain.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. The drive distance keeps casual Denver weekenders away.
metadata.logistics_notes: About a 4 to 4.5 hour drive from Denver depending on weather.

metadata.reputation: 100% all-natural snow. A true local's gem straddling the Continental Divide.
metadata.terrain_character: Mirkwood basin offers incredible hike-to powder. Very fun, rolling natural terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredible snow preservation due to high base elevation and zero snowmaking.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low midweek, but tiny base area creates severe bottlenecks on powder weekends when locals flock there.
metadata.logistics_notes: About a 3 to 3.5 hour drive from Denver.

logistics.drive_notes: Requires driving all the way past Glenwood Springs to the Grand Mesa.
logistics.lodging_notes: Grand Junction or Palisade are the closest major towns, about 45 mins away.

metadata.reputation: Unique, flat-topped mountain riding on the edge of the desert.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible glade skiing and boulder drops. Very flat on top, but steeper at the bottom.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unique western flow storms that miss the rest of Colorado.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Very isolated from the I-70 corridor crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great option if a massive storm hits the far western edge of the state.

logistics.drive_notes: Highway 210 is a notorious avalanche corridor. The 'Red Snake' morning traffic is brutal. Strict traction laws.
logistics.lodging_notes: Stay in SLC or Sandy for budget options. On-mountain at Alta/Bird is extremely limited and expensive.

metadata.reputation: The king of Utah freeride. Massive, steep, and aggressive.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible sustained steeps, huge bowls (Mineral Basin), and legendary tram laps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive lake-effect dumps. Light snow, but gets tracked out quickly by expert crowds.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extreme on powder days. Tram lines can be massive.
metadata.logistics_notes: Interlodge (being locked inside due to avalanche danger) is a real risk during massive dumps.

metadata.reputation: Old school, skiers-only legendary traverse-accessed powder.
metadata.terrain_character: Unbelievable micro-terrain, natural features, and steep tree shots.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Historically the highest snowfall in Utah. Perfect cold smoke.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on powder days, but traverses organically spread people out.
metadata.logistics_notes: NO SNOWBOARDING ALLOWED.

logistics.drive_notes: Highway 190 traffic is awful on weekends. Required winter tires/chains.
logistics.lodging_notes: Cottonwood Heights offers great access and cheaper lodging.

metadata.reputation: Local's favorite, massive snowboard culture, playful terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Perfectly gladed trees, natural side-hits everywhere, great terrain parks.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very high base elevation means it stays incredibly cold and light.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high, attracts a younger, aggressive local crowd.
metadata.logistics_notes: Parking reservations strictly enforced. Great night skiing.

metadata.reputation: The 'quiet' Cottonwood resort, though less quiet since joining Ikon.
metadata.terrain_character: Honeycomb Canyon offers incredible steep, open powder runs and hike-to terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great snow, but lower mountain can get sun-baked faster than Brighton.
metadata.crowd_factor: Slightly lower than Brighton/Snowbird, but still busy on powder days.
metadata.logistics_notes: Paid parking requires reservations.

logistics.drive_notes: I-80 route is a massive multi-lane highway, much easier and less prone to closure than the Cottonwood Canyons.
logistics.lodging_notes: Park City is expensive luxury lodging. Kimball Junction is slightly cheaper.

metadata.reputation: Sprawling, luxury mega-resort attached to an incredible town.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless cruisers. The Canyons side (Ninety-Nine 90) hides excellent steep tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower base elevation can mean heavier snow or rain risks during warmer storms.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely high. Lift lines at bases can be brutal, but the acreage absorbs people.
metadata.logistics_notes: Town lift goes right into Main Street for unparalleled apres access.

metadata.reputation: Ultra-luxury, impeccable grooming, and a skiers-only strict policy.
metadata.terrain_character: Mostly pristine groomers, but the Empire area offers excellent steep tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Similar to Park City, the lower elevation requires monitoring for rain/mixed precip.
metadata.crowd_factor: Capped daily ticket sales keep crowds manageable.
metadata.logistics_notes: NO SNOWBOARDING ALLOWED.

metadata.reputation: Quiet, scenic resort founded by Robert Redford. Low acreage but steep vertical.
metadata.terrain_character: Steep, heavily treed, and rugged. Beautiful views of Mt. Timpanogos.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very low base elevation makes it highly susceptible to rain/warm weather.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. Ignored by the mega-pass crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located south of the Cottonwoods in Provo Canyon.

logistics.drive_notes: Easy drive north of SLC on I-15. Avoids all canyon gridlock of LCC/BCC.
logistics.lodging_notes: Ogden offers affordable city lodging. Huntsville has quiet, local options.

metadata.reputation: Olympic downhill runs and incredibly opulent day lodges.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive vertical drop, wide open bowls, and long, fast steep cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower base elevation (6,391 ft) means rain is a threat on the bottom half.
metadata.crowd_factor: Noticeably less crowded than the Cottonwood or Park City resorts.
metadata.logistics_notes: Massive free parking lots with shuttles.

metadata.reputation: Massive sprawling acreage, very old-school, no artificial snowmaking.
metadata.terrain_character: Very flat in spots. Endless mellow tree glades. Highly reliant on snowcat or bus access for certain zones.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: 100% natural snow. Excellent preservation due to low skier density.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Capped daily lift tickets ensure powder lasts for days.
metadata.logistics_notes: Recently went semi-private, restricting some terrain for real estate owners only.

metadata.reputation: Family-owned, deeply local mountain near the Idaho border.
metadata.terrain_character: Great mix of rolling groomers and fun tree stashes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Far north location catches great cold storms.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Mostly college students from Logan.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very remote from SLC.

logistics.drive_notes: Long drive through the desert, but highly scenic and avoids SLC canyon traffic.
logistics.lodging_notes: Beaver, UT provides access. Very remote.

metadata.reputation: Tiny, isolated gem that only opens Friday-Monday.
metadata.terrain_character: Steep trees on the backside, mellow cruisers on the front.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very high elevation guarantees cold, dry snow. Untracked Tuesday-Thursday powder sits waiting for Friday opening.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only operates 4 days a week. Plan accordingly.

metadata.reputation: Highest base elevation in Utah. A weird, wonderful southern anomaly.
metadata.terrain_character: Mellow pitch across two peaks. Famous for skiing past red-rock hoodoos (like Bryce Canyon).
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very high elevation keeps the snow incredibly dry.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Gets weekend traffic from Las Vegas.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only 3 hours from Vegas, but catches huge southern storm tracks that completely miss the Cottonwood Canyons.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to RNO is mandatory from Denver. I-80 (Donner Pass) frequently closes during storms. Turo with 3PMSF tires is essential.
logistics.lodging_notes: Truckee is the hub. Staying north of the lake is mandatory to avoid Highway 89 avalanche closures.

metadata.reputation: The mecca of extreme freeride and cliff drops. Home of 'Squallywood'.
metadata.terrain_character: KT-22 is arguably the best chairlift in North America. Insane steeps, chutes, and huckable cliffs on the Olympic side. Alpine Meadows adds incredible wide-open bowls, gullies, and hike-to terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Base elevation is 6,200ft. 'Sierra Cement' is real, and the base frequently sees rain while the summit dumps. Alpine Meadows side sits slightly higher.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extreme. Bay Area weekend traffic creates massive lift lines and gridlock.
metadata.logistics_notes: Connected via Base-to-Base Gondola between Olympic Valley and Alpine Meadows. Alpine Meadows side can close heavily for avalanche control.

metadata.reputation: Family-friendly, luxury groomers, and world-class terrain parks. Nicknamed 'Flatstar'.
metadata.terrain_character: Very cruisy. Excellent tree skiing (Martis Camp) that is highly protected from the wind during major storms.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower base. Snow is often heavier.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great fallback option when high winds close Palisades lifts.

metadata.reputation: Old-school vibe. Consistently records the highest snow totals in North Tahoe.
metadata.terrain_character: Steep, rugged, and fun. Great tree skiing off Mt. Judah.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Sits directly on Donner Pass, catching the absolute brunt of Pacific storms.
metadata.crowd_factor: Lower than Palisades/Northstar, but still busy on powder days.
metadata.logistics_notes: Because it's on Donner Pass, you are highly likely to get snowed-in or out during major events.

metadata.reputation: The highest base elevation in Tahoe. The ultimate insurance policy against rain.
metadata.terrain_character: The Chutes offer 1,000+ vertical feet of sustained, incredibly steep pitch (40-55 degrees).
metadata.snow_quality_notes: When the rest of Tahoe is getting rained on, Mt. Rose is getting cold, dry snow.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. A local favorite for Reno residents.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only 25 minutes from Reno Airport (RNO).

metadata.reputation: Incredible, uninterrupted views of Lake Tahoe directly from the runs.
metadata.terrain_character: Phenomenal, widely-spaced tree skiing. Great sustained fall lines.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very low elevation right on the lake. Highly susceptible to heavy snow and rain.
metadata.logistics_notes: Highway 89 access can be difficult during storms.

metadata.reputation: Community-owned resort in Incline Village. Small but steep.
metadata.terrain_character: Great glades and steep cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Protected from the heaviest winds. Snow preserves well.
metadata.logistics_notes: Technically in Nevada. Great backup option when I-80 is a mess.

logistics.drive_notes: Highway 50 (from Sacramento) or NV-207 (Kingsbury Grade) from Reno. Do NOT expect to travel between North and South Tahoe during a storm due to Hwy 89 Emerald Bay closures.
logistics.lodging_notes: Stateline NV offers massive casino-hotel options. South Lake Tahoe has endless budget motels.

metadata.reputation: Massive footprint straddling the CA/NV border. Unbeatable lake views.
metadata.terrain_character: World-class tree skiing (Mott Canyon, Killebrew Canyon) and endless rolling cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Massive elevation split. The base will often be pouring rain while the upper mountain receives 2 feet of snow.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high, but spreads out decently due to sheer size.
metadata.logistics_notes: High winds constantly close the main gondola and upper lifts.

metadata.reputation: The undisputed hardcore freeride mountain of South Tahoe.
metadata.terrain_character: Insanely steep gullies, massive natural cliff drops (The Wall), and perfect cornices.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High base elevation (7,800ft) and unique geography means it catches drastically more snow than Heavenly, and keeps it colder.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on powder days, full of aggressive locals.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located 45 mins south of Lake Tahoe. Highway 88 closures (Spur) happen almost every major storm. You must stay in Kirkwood to guarantee access on a powder day.

metadata.reputation: Massive local snowboard culture. Playful, fun terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Historically famous for tree skiing, though heavily impacted by the recent Caldor Fire. Incredible parks.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Classic heavy Sierra snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located on Highway 50.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Mammoth (MMH) or Reno (RNO) + 3hr drive south. Highway 395 is gorgeous but exposed to extreme winds.
logistics.lodging_notes: Mammoth Lakes offers everything from hostels to luxury condos.

metadata.reputation: A massive, high-alpine volcano with a season that frequently stretches into July or August.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible steep drops off the top (Cornice Bowl, Climax), massive parks, and great glades on the lower half.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High elevation preserves snow incredibly well. The wind-buff off the summit is world famous.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high. The primary destination for millions of Southern California skiers.
metadata.logistics_notes: Top gondola closes frequently due to extreme summit winds (100+ mph).

metadata.reputation: Mammoth's quiet, family-friendly little sister.
metadata.terrain_character: Wide, rolling groomers. Great, perfectly spaced trees (especially off J7).
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Excellent snow, completely protected from the brutal winds that blast Mammoth.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Empty even when Mammoth is packed.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only 25 minutes north of Mammoth. The perfect audible call if Mammoth's lifts are on wind hold.

logistics.drive_notes: Requires driving up winding, low-elevation Sierra foothills. Chain control is absolute.
logistics.lodging_notes: Lodging is localized and limited compared to Tahoe.

metadata.reputation: Quiet, mid-Sierra gem on Highway 4.
metadata.terrain_character: Grizzly Bowl offers surprisingly rugged, steep freeride drops.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Classic heavy Sierra snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Hwy 4 closes east of the resort in winter.

metadata.reputation: Family resort for the Central Valley.
metadata.terrain_character: Great glades and natural rolling terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower elevation, highly dependent on storm temps.
metadata.logistics_notes: Closest skiing to the Bay Area/Central Valley via Hwy 108.

metadata.reputation: Fresno's local mountain, incredibly fun terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Steep rocky drops, great glades, very playful.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Can get rained out, but holds snow well on north-facing aspects.
metadata.logistics_notes: Hwy 168 access.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to SEA required. I-90 and US-2 are notorious for severe winter closures. 3PMSF winter tires are mandatory.
logistics.lodging_notes: Staying 'on pass' is highly recommended to avoid being locked out behind highway avalanche gates during a major storm.

metadata.reputation: Playful, heavily-featured terrain with a massive night skiing culture.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible natural side-hits, steep tree glades on the backside (Mill Valley), and heavy, wet 'Cascade Concrete' snow.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Low elevation means rain is a constant threat at the base. Snow is dense and heavy.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extreme. US-2 is a two-lane highway that suffers from severe gridlock on powder weekends.
metadata.logistics_notes: Parking fills up incredibly early. Night riding is often the best way to avoid crowds.

metadata.reputation: A tiny, fiercely steep micro-resort famous for its rugged backcountry gates.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredibly steep, sustained fall lines and extreme cliff drops in the backcountry.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely low base elevation (3,140 ft). Rain and heavy, wet snow are common.
metadata.crowd_factor: High. The small footprint means it gets tracked out almost instantly.
metadata.logistics_notes: Right off I-90, making it very accessible but highly vulnerable to pass closures.

metadata.reputation: Seattle's closest local hill. Very flat, heavily beginner-focused.
metadata.terrain_character: Mostly rolling groomers and massive terrain parks.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Base is under 3,000 ft. Freezing levels are the biggest issue here.
metadata.logistics_notes: Directly adjacent to I-90.

logistics.drive_notes: Highway 410 (to Crystal) can experience severe delays or closures due to avalanches.
logistics.lodging_notes: Greenwater offers cabin rentals; staying near the resort is highly advised during active storms.

metadata.reputation: The premier big-mountain resort in Washington. Massive alpine terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible alpine bowls, steep chutes (Silver King), and excellent lower-mountain tree glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Slightly higher base than Stevens/Snoqualmie, meaning it holds colder snow better.
metadata.logistics_notes: The upper mountain gondola and high-alpine chairs are frequently closed during active storms due to avalanche danger.

metadata.reputation: Quiet, family-friendly resort south of Mt. Rainier.
metadata.terrain_character: Great tree skiing in the Paradise Basin area. Mellow pitch overall.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Good elevation helps preserve snow quality.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. Ignored by the Seattle mega-pass crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Much more relaxed vibe than Crystal or Stevens.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Bellingham (BLI) or Seattle (SEA) + long drive north. Highway 542 is the only way in.
logistics.lodging_notes: No on-mountain lodging. Stay in Glacier or Bellingham.

metadata.reputation: World record holder for most snowfall in a season (1,140 inches). Hardcore, ungroomed freeride.
metadata.terrain_character: Extremely rugged, steep natural terrain. Incredible backcountry access.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very low elevation. Rain is a constant threat, but when it dumps cold snow, it's bottomless.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Attracts purists from all over the PNW.
metadata.logistics_notes: Zero luxury. Old lifts, rustic lodges. A true snowboarder's paradise.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Spokane (GEG). Much drier climate than the Cascades.
logistics.lodging_notes: Spokane or Wenatchee serve as great hubs.

metadata.reputation: Huge acreage, incredibly quiet, old-school vibe.
metadata.terrain_character: Wide open cruisers and excellent glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Drier, lighter snow than the coastal Cascades.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located just north of Spokane.

metadata.reputation: Famous for sunshine and drier snow on the eastern side of the Cascades.
metadata.terrain_character: Great off-piste bowls and widely spaced trees.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lighter, fluffier snow than the west side, but lower overall totals.
metadata.logistics_notes: Near Wenatchee, WA.

metadata.reputation: Spokane's local mountain, great night skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: Rolling terrain, solid tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Good interior snow quality, heavily protected by trees.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very easy drive from Spokane airport.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Portland (PDX). Highway 26 can get nasty during storms.
logistics.lodging_notes: Government Camp provides great local access. Portland is only 1.5 hours away.

metadata.reputation: The premier big-mountain freeride destination on Mt. Hood.
metadata.terrain_character: Heather Canyon offers incredibly steep, natural halfpipes and extreme gullies.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Dense coastal snow. High winds frequently scour the upper mountain.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on weekends due to Portland day-trippers.
metadata.logistics_notes: Upper lifts close frequently due to severe weather and rime ice.

metadata.reputation: Famous historic lodge and the longest ski season in North America (summer glacier skiing).
metadata.terrain_character: Very flat, cruisy terrain. The Palmer snowfield offers high alpine riding when open.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great upper elevation, but the terrain lacks pitch.
metadata.logistics_notes: Merged with Summit pass area to dramatically increase overall vertical drop.

metadata.reputation: The steepest night skiing in America.
metadata.terrain_character: Surprisingly steep and rugged terrain for its size. The Upper Bowl is legendary.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely low elevation makes it highly susceptible to rain.
metadata.logistics_notes: The ultimate fallback option when the higher Mt. Hood resorts are closed for wind.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Redmond (RDM) or PDX + 3.5 hour drive.
logistics.lodging_notes: Bend, OR is an incredible ski town with great breweries and lodging.

metadata.reputation: A massive, perfectly conical volcano. A snowboarder's paradise.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless wind-lips, natural quarterpipes, and surfy, 360-degree rideable terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Drier snow than Mt. Hood. Rime ice at the summit is brutal.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. High acreage swallows crowds easily.
metadata.logistics_notes: The Summit chair is the best terrain on the mountain but is closed roughly 50% of the winter due to extreme winds.

metadata.reputation: Deep local gem with cheap tickets and a massive RV parking culture.
metadata.terrain_character: Fun, rolling bowls and great trees.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Classic heavy PNW snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great spot if Bachelor is completely winded out.

metadata.reputation: Steep, aggressive local mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: RTS is one of the steepest lift-accessed runs in Oregon.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Decent elevation keeps snow fairly protected.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located further south, catching different storm flows.

logistics.drive_notes: Highly remote. Driving from Boise (BOI) is the easiest access point.
logistics.lodging_notes: Baker City is the nearest civilization.

metadata.reputation: Highest base elevation in Oregon. A pure, untracked powder oasis.
metadata.terrain_character: Only one chairlift, but massive acreage of perfectly gladed powder bowls.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: The snow here is incredibly light and dry compared to the Cascades.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only open Thursday through Sunday.

logistics.drive_notes: An 8.5 hour extreme drive from Denver, or a flight into JAC. Winter driving over Teton Pass or through the Snake River Canyon is extremely hazardous.
logistics.lodging_notes: Jackson is luxury pricing. Driggs/Victor (Idaho side) is much more affordable but requires driving Teton Pass.

metadata.reputation: One of the most legendary extreme freeride mountains on earth.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible sustained steeps, massive vertical drop, and world-class backcountry gates.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Upper mountain is incredible; lower mountain can suffer from rain/melt due to the 6,300ft base.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high. The tram line gets massive on powder days.
metadata.logistics_notes: Avalanche gear (beacon, shovel, probe) is strictly required to exit the backcountry gates.

metadata.reputation: The 'back side' of the Tetons. Flat, but catches unbelievable amounts of snow.
metadata.terrain_character: Very mellow pitch. Open powder fields and widely spaced trees. Perfect for low-stress cruising.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Consistently gets vastly more snow than Jackson Hole due to its western-facing aspect.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Overshadowed by Jackson Hole.
metadata.logistics_notes: Accessed from the Idaho side (Driggs). Often completely fogged in (nicknamed 'Grand Foggee').

metadata.reputation: The 'Town Hill' of Jackson.
metadata.terrain_character: Very steep, straight fall lines dropping right into town.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Same base elevation issues as Jackson Hole.
metadata.logistics_notes: Literally blocks from the town square in Jackson.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Bozeman (BZN).
logistics.lodging_notes: Bozeman is a great hub. Big Sky resort lodging is extremely expensive.

metadata.reputation: The sprawling giant of American skiing. Massive, rugged, and rocky.
metadata.terrain_character: Lone Peak Tram offers European-style extreme alpine skiing. The rest of the mountain is endless cruisers and glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very rocky terrain that requires a massive base depth to ski safely off-piste.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. It is getting busier, but 5,850 acres absorbs a lot of people.
metadata.logistics_notes: Tram access now requires an expensive add-on fee, even for pass holders.

metadata.reputation: A fiercely guarded local non-profit mountain with insane expert terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: The Ridge offers some of the steepest, most technical inbounds hike-to chutes in North America.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unique cold-smoke powder ('Bozeman Cold Smoke').
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Very heavy local college crowd (MSU).
metadata.logistics_notes: Avalanche transceiver is strictly required to ride the Schlasman's lift or hike The Ridge.

metadata.reputation: The world's only private ski and golf community.
metadata.terrain_character: Groomers and powder fields groomed to perfection.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Pristine.
metadata.logistics_notes: PRIVATE RESORT. Do not alert unless the user owns a $10M home here.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight into Kalispell (FCA) or Missoula (MSO).
logistics.lodging_notes: Whitefish is an excellent ski town. Missoula offers great access to the smaller gems.

metadata.reputation: Massive resort famous for its 'Snow Ghosts' (heavily rime-encrusted trees).
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible, massive tree glades. Very little extreme alpine, but endless rolling deep powder.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Plagued by heavy fog inversions (nicknamed 'White-out-fish').
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Highly popular but very large.
metadata.logistics_notes: Riding here on a powder day often requires navigating by the trees due to zero visibility.

metadata.reputation: Missoula's steep, rugged local mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: Famous for long, continuous steep pitches and incredible glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Often catches very dry inland snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Only 20 minutes from downtown Missoula.

metadata.reputation: The hidden gem of Montana.
metadata.terrain_character: The backside of this mountain features some of the best, uncrowded steep lift-accessed trees in the state.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High base elevation holds snow wonderfully.
metadata.logistics_notes: Near Philipsburg, MT.

metadata.reputation: Old school powder haven on the MT/ID border.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible glades and rolling powder fields.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive storms funneling up the Bitterroot Valley.
metadata.logistics_notes: Open Thursday through Sunday only. Thursday 'powder days' here are legendary.

metadata.reputation: Eastern Montana's big mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: Great sustained pitches, beautiful views, and good trees.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Vulnerable to wind, but holds cold snow well.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located south of Billings.

metadata.reputation: Helena's local hill. Fiercely independent.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive acreage for a local hill. Endless glades and hidden stashes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great cold, dry interior snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Often the first ski area in Montana to open each year.

metadata.reputation: An 'upside-down' mountain with stunning views of Flathead Lake.
metadata.terrain_character: You park at the top and ski down to the lifts. Great glades and cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Excellent natural snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located just south of Whitefish, MT.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Spokane, WA (GEG) and drive east into Idaho.
logistics.lodging_notes: Sandpoint, ID is a gorgeous lake town with access to Schweitzer.

metadata.reputation: Massive, highly underrated resort with views of Lake Pend Oreille.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible sustained fall line tree skiing and massive open bowls.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Prone to the same heavy fog inversions as Whitefish. Snow is slightly heavier than the Rockies but lighter than the Cascades.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very isolated from major population centers, making it a great strike mission.

metadata.reputation: Gondola-accessed resort right over I-90.
metadata.terrain_character: Great glades and fun, rolling cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Excellent snow preservation.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires taking the longest single-stage gondola in North America just to reach the base area.

metadata.reputation: Tiny footprint, massive snowfall.
metadata.terrain_character: Very heavily gladed.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches over 400 inches of snow annually, making it one of the most reliable powder spots in Idaho.
metadata.logistics_notes: Straddles the ID/MT border directly on I-90.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Boise (BOI) or Sun Valley (SUN).
logistics.lodging_notes: Boise offers city convenience, while McCall and Ketchum offer true ski-town vibes.

metadata.reputation: America's first destination ski resort. Pure luxury and perfectly pitched cruisers.
metadata.terrain_character: Relentless, perfectly pitched, steep cruisers. Very little flat terrain on Bald Mountain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Relies very heavily on the world's largest automated snowmaking system, as natural snowfall is lower here.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. High-speed lift network keeps lines very short.
metadata.logistics_notes: Not a primary target for deep powder chasing compared to the Panhandle.

metadata.reputation: Boise's massive non-profit local mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible 360-degree riding off the summit. Fantastic glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Relies entirely on natural snow. Very vulnerable during dry/warm years.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on weekends due to Boise proximity.
metadata.logistics_notes: Winding, 45-minute drive up the mountain from downtown Boise.

metadata.reputation: Hidden powder gem in central Idaho.
metadata.terrain_character: Famous for its perfectly spaced tree skiing and rolling powder fields.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive amounts of light, dry snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located near McCall, ID.

metadata.reputation: A reborn luxury resort with fantastic fall lines.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible, sustained steep groomers and great glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Good snow, but lower base elevation than Brundage.
metadata.logistics_notes: Also located near McCall, ID.

metadata.reputation: A shockingly steep local mountain in southern Idaho.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible, sustained steep fall lines right off the summit.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High elevation keeps it cold, but highly reliant on natural dumps.
metadata.logistics_notes: Just outside Pocatello, ID.

metadata.reputation: Tiny, beloved local hill (once owned by Bruce Willis).
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible, uncrowded glades and rolling natural powder fields.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great interior Idaho snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Also offers highly affordable cat-skiing operations right next to the resort.

logistics.drive_notes: A relatively easy drive south from Denver on I-25. Generally avoids the brutal gridlock of I-70.
logistics.lodging_notes: Taos has limited on-mountain lodging; the town of Taos (30 mins away) is the primary budget hub. Santa Fe offers great city lodging.

metadata.reputation: A fiercely steep, expert-focused mountain with European alpine vibes.
metadata.terrain_character: Kachina Peak (now lift-accessed) and the surrounding ridge hikes offer some of the most technical, steep chutes in North America.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very high base elevation preserves snow perfectly. Catches incredible southern storm wrapping.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. Very isolated compared to major Colorado resorts.
metadata.logistics_notes: Recently lifted its ban on snowboarding (2008). Requires a massive base to cover the sharp rocks.

metadata.reputation: One of the highest ski areas in the country.
metadata.terrain_character: Great tree glades (Big Tesuque) and fun rolling cruisers. Lacks extreme steeps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredibly high base elevation (10,350ft) ensures pristine, dry cold-smoke powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. Mostly locals from Santa Fe and Albuquerque.
metadata.logistics_notes: The winding 16-mile mountain road up from Santa Fe can be treacherous during storms.

metadata.reputation: Family-friendly alternative to Taos.
metadata.terrain_character: Mostly intermediate cruisers and great glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Does not catch the extreme totals that Taos does, but holds what it gets well.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located on the 'back side' of the mountains from Taos.

metadata.reputation: A tiny ski-in/ski-out resort literally dropping right into main street.
metadata.terrain_character: Short, surprisingly steep fall lines.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Relies heavily on snowmaking during dry spells, but great during a big dump.
metadata.logistics_notes: You can walk from your hotel to the lift.

logistics.drive_notes: Long drive south down I-25. Very desolate stretches of highway.
logistics.lodging_notes: Ruidoso serves as the main hub for Ski Apache.

metadata.reputation: Owned and operated by the Mescalero Apache Tribe.
metadata.terrain_character: Surprising massive bowl skiing and good tree spacing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Highly volatile. Either catches huge southern dumps or suffers terrible dry spells.
metadata.logistics_notes: The southernmost major ski area in the U.S.

metadata.reputation: Albuquerque's local backdrop.
metadata.terrain_character: Mellow cruisers. Accessed via an incredible tramway.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely volatile. Needs a massive storm to open.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great novelty if an unusual southern storm pounds ABQ.

logistics.drive_notes: Drive is over 11 hours. Flying into PHX and driving 2.5 hours north is preferred.
logistics.lodging_notes: Flagstaff is an incredible mountain town with endless lodging options.

metadata.reputation: Massive alpine riding in the desert.
metadata.terrain_character: Sits on the flanks of the highest peak in AZ. Incredible open bowl skiing above treeline and tight glades below.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unbelievable, massive dumps when El Nino storm tracks dip south.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on weekends when Phoenix locals drive up.
metadata.logistics_notes: The access road is incredibly winding and heavily policed during storms.

metadata.reputation: Arizona's largest ski area, owned by the White Mountain Apache Tribe.
metadata.terrain_character: Spread across three peaks. Excellent, mellow tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great snow preservation due to high elevation.
metadata.logistics_notes: Highly remote, located near the NM border in eastern AZ.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Anchorage (ANC) is absolute necessity. 45-minute drive south to Alyeska on the Seward Highway.
logistics.lodging_notes: Alyeska Resort has a luxury hotel at the base, or you can stay in Girdwood.

metadata.reputation: Incredible maritime snow totals and true Alaskan big-mountain riding.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive, steep alpine bowls, heavy cliff features, and incredibly rugged terrain on the North Face.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: True maritime snow. The base is practically at sea level (250 ft), so rain is common at the bottom while it dumps 4 feet up top.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very dark mid-winter; late February/March is the premier strike window.

metadata.reputation: Juneau's community-owned, no-frills powder gem.
metadata.terrain_character: Steep, heavily gladed, and completely uncrowded.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredible coastal dumps, but highly susceptible to rain.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires flying into Juneau (JNU), which is inaccessible by car from the mainland.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Houghton (CMX) or massive drive through the Midwest.
logistics.lodging_notes: On-site yurts and a hostel. Very rustic.

metadata.reputation: The Midwest anomaly. No grooming, no beginners allowed, extreme lake-effect powder.
metadata.terrain_character: Entirely natural, tight tree glades and steep boulder drops. Completely un-groomed.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches nearly 300 inches of pure Lake Superior lake-effect snow.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. The extreme location keeps casuals away.
metadata.logistics_notes: There are zero beginner runs. A shuttle bus picks you up at the bottom of the runs along the lake road and drives you back to the lift.

logistics.drive_notes: Flight to Burlington (BTV). Only trigger this trip if the Western U.S. is completely dry and a massive Nor'easter stalls over New England.
logistics.lodging_notes: Burlington, Waterbury, or Stowe offer great hubs. Driving between resorts in winter can be slow going.

metadata.reputation: The undisputed powder king of the East Coast.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredibly steep, tight tree glades. Some of the best glade skiing in North America.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches the 'Jay Cloud' anomaly, dropping 350+ inches of snow annually (massive for the East).
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Its extreme northern location (right on the Canadian border) keeps casuals away.
metadata.logistics_notes: High winds frequently shut down the iconic aerial tram.

metadata.reputation: The luxury capital of East Coast skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: The 'Front Four' runs are legendary, steep, double-fall-line plunges. Great tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Relies heavily on snowmaking, but catches great natural snow during Nor'easters.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely high on weekends. Heavy Boston/NY traffic.
metadata.logistics_notes: The mountain road from the town of Stowe is notorious for traffic backups.

metadata.reputation: Rugged, old-school, family-friendly resort.
metadata.terrain_character: Madonna Mountain offers incredibly steep, narrow, natural terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great snow preservation. Very rustic.
metadata.logistics_notes: The road connecting Stowe to Smugglers' Notch closes in winter. You must drive around.

metadata.reputation: Classic Vermont skiing with massive vertical and beautiful terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Castlerock Peak is legendary for steep, narrow, natural-snow-only runs.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great natural snow, highly susceptible to freeze/thaw cycles.
metadata.logistics_notes: Divided into two separate base areas (Lincoln Peak and Mt. Ellen) connected by a chairlift.

metadata.reputation: The 'Beast of the East'. Sprawling, massive, and aggressive.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless runs across multiple peaks. Steep bump runs on Bear Mountain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: The most aggressive snowmaking in the world. Season often lasts until June.
metadata.crowd_factor: Massive. The busiest resort on the East Coast.
metadata.logistics_notes: A massive party town. Great for an East Coast strike mission if a storm hits central VT.

metadata.reputation: Killington's quiet, old-school little sister.
metadata.terrain_character: Classic, narrow, winding New England trails.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Holds natural snow well due to fewer skiers scraping it off.
metadata.logistics_notes: A great bailout option when Killington is too crowded.

metadata.reputation: Ski it if you can. A fierce, all-natural, independent cooperative.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredibly steep, narrow, rocky, and completely un-groomed. The single chair is legendary.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Relies 100% on natural snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: NO SNOWBOARDING ALLOWED.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Portland, ME (PWM) or Albany, NY (ALB). Very remote driving.
logistics.lodging_notes: Lodging is highly isolated near the specific mountains.

metadata.reputation: The only true above-treeline snowfields in the East.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive, continuous pitch. The snowfields at the summit offer Rocky Mountain style open bowls.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredibly cold. High winds frequently scour the top.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Its remote location filters out the casual Boston crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires a massive natural snow base to open the summit snowfields.

metadata.reputation: A reborn independent legend. Raw, rugged, and completely uncrowded.
metadata.terrain_character: The Kennebago Steeps offer some of the best, most sustained expert terrain in New England.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High base elevation means excellent natural snow preservation.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located in Rangeley, ME. Deep in the wilderness.

metadata.reputation: Sprawling, interconnected peaks with legendary snowmaking.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless rolling cruisers. White Heat is famous as a massive, steep, double-fall-line bump run.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Highly volatile natural snow, but the snowmaking is world-class.
metadata.logistics_notes: A massive resort that requires planning to navigate across its 8 peaks.

metadata.reputation: Host of the 1980 Olympics. Nicknamed 'Iceface'.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredibly steep, sustained pitch. The Slides are out-of-bounds style extreme chutes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: When it's cold, it's a sheer sheet of ice. When it dumps 3 feet of powder, it is unmatched.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located in the Adirondacks (NY). The Slides are rarely open due to strict base depth requirements.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Vancouver (YVR). Beautiful, easy 2-hour drive up the Sea-to-Sky Highway. Passports required.
logistics.lodging_notes: Whistler Village is sprawling and very expensive.

metadata.reputation: The largest resort in North America. An absolute giant.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible high-alpine glaciers, massive bowls, and deep coastal tree glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Base elevation is incredibly low (2,140 ft). It is very common for it to rain in the village while dumping 2 feet of snow in the alpine.
metadata.logistics_notes: Connected by the incredible Peak 2 Peak Gondola.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Kelowna (YLW), Calgary (YYC), or Spokane (GEG). Driving the Powder Highway in winter requires absolute confidence in severe winter driving.
logistics.lodging_notes: Towns like Revelstoke, Golden, and Fernie offer amazing, rustic ski-town lodging.

metadata.reputation: The most vertical drop in North America. Hardcore, steep, and deep.
metadata.terrain_character: Relentless, incredibly steep, perfectly spaced tree glades and massive high-alpine bowls.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive interior BC dumps. The massive vertical means you can ski from deep dry powder down into heavy wet snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Lifts are laid out in a massive vertical stack. Very little beginner terrain.

metadata.reputation: The 'Champagne Powder Capital of Canada'.
metadata.terrain_character: Four massive, incredibly rugged alpine bowls that funnel down into steep chutes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Very dry, light snow. Highly susceptible to wind scour on the ridges.
metadata.logistics_notes: Almost entirely accessed by a single base-to-summit gondola, which can create morning bottlenecks.

metadata.reputation: Famous for its five massive, sprawling alpine bowls.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless ridges, saddles, and drops spread across the bowls.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Prone to the 'Fernie Factor' — unpredictable weather that can drop 3 feet of powder or unexpected rain.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located far to the south, making it a viable (but long) drive from Montana/Idaho.

metadata.reputation: The purest soul-skiing tree destination in Canada.
metadata.terrain_character: 360-degree fall lines off multiple peaks. Incredible glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Deep, reliable interior snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Near Rossland, BC. A hidden gem that recently joined Ikon.

metadata.reputation: A Powder Highway staple with massive vertical drop.
metadata.terrain_character: Taynton Bowl offers 1,000+ acres of ex-heli-skiing terrain. Incredibly steep and deep.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Excellent dry snow, but the lower mountain can occasionally get icy due to the sheer vertical drop.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very remote. Often grouped into a multi-resort Powder Highway road trip.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Calgary (YYC). Very easy, flat 1.5-hour drive on the Trans-Canada Highway.
logistics.lodging_notes: Banff is a bustling, gorgeous national park town. Very strict development laws keep it pristine.

metadata.reputation: Jaw-dropping scenery and incredible, rugged back bowls.
metadata.terrain_character: The front side is massive rolling cruisers; the back bowls are extreme, steep, and rocky.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: The coldest region on this list. Snow is incredibly dry, but total volume is lower than interior BC.
metadata.logistics_notes: Temperatures can easily hit -20F in January.

metadata.reputation: High alpine plateau with incredible freeride zones.
metadata.terrain_character: Rolling alpine above-treeline. 'Delirium Dive' is a gated, insanely steep, rocky extreme freeride zone.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: 100% natural snow. Catches more volume than Lake Louise due to its location on the Continental Divide.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires taking a 20-minute gondola just to reach the base village. Avalanche gear strictly required for Delirium Dive.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly to Boston (BOS) or Albany (ALB). Major winter Nor'easters make I-89 and I-91 very treacherous.
logistics.lodging_notes: Southern Vermont is heavily populated with resort lodging, but books up instantly during storms.

metadata.reputation: Luxury resort with a massive village. Heavily favored by New York City weekenders.
metadata.terrain_character: Wide, heavily groomed cruisers. Decent tree glades when natural snow allows.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredible snowmaking, but highly reliant on natural dumps for the trees to open.
metadata.logistics_notes: Birthplace of the modern snowboard (Jake Burton Carpenter).

metadata.reputation: The closest major Vermont resort to the major East Coast cities.
metadata.terrain_character: Carinthia face is an entirely separate mountain dedicated to massive terrain parks.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Southern location means it occasionally gets rain when Northern VT gets snow.
metadata.logistics_notes: Easy drive from Boston or NYC.

metadata.reputation: Impeccable grooming and family-focused luxury.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless rolling intermediate terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Known for the best grooming in the East.
metadata.logistics_notes: Lacks the extreme steeps of Stowe or Jay Peak.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Ontario (ONT) or LAX. Driving up the mountain roads from LA during a snowstorm is a true nightmare of gridlock and unprepared drivers.
logistics.lodging_notes: Big Bear Lake has plenty of cabins, but books up instantly when snow hits.

metadata.reputation: The park capital of Southern California.
metadata.terrain_character: Very small footprint, almost entirely dedicated to massive terrain parks and halfpipes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Usually man-made slush, but occasionally gets 5+ foot dumps from atmospheric rivers.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extreme. LA weekend traffic is brutal.
metadata.logistics_notes: A fun novelty if you want to surf in Malibu and snowboard in the same day.

metadata.reputation: A shockingly steep, rugged, old-school mountain looming right over Los Angeles.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredibly steep chutes, tight trees, and un-groomed natural terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Highly volatile. It often sits closed for weeks waiting for a massive storm, then opens with epic natural powder.
metadata.logistics_notes: If it actually dumps snow here, the steep terrain is legitimately world-class, but the access road gets treacherous.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Redding (RDD) or Medford, OR (MFR). Very remote section of I-5.
logistics.lodging_notes: The town of Mt. Shasta offers affordable, quiet lodging.

metadata.reputation: A quiet, spectacular volcano isolated from the Tahoe and Cascades crowds.
metadata.terrain_character: Great rolling cruisers and excellent backcountry access off the summit.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive, wet Pacific storms.
metadata.logistics_notes: An incredible alternative when Tahoe is suffering from massive traffic gridlock.

logistics.drive_notes: Avoids I-70 entirely! Take Highway 119 through Boulder/Nederland.
logistics.lodging_notes: Boulder or Nederland offer immediate access.

metadata.reputation: Boulder's fiercely windy local hill that catches freak upslope blizzards.
metadata.terrain_character: Great, steep tree skiing in the Corona Bowl area when the wind hasn't scoured it.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely vulnerable to wind. Only target this mountain during 'Upslope' storms that blow from east to west.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high on weekends due to Boulder college crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Parking frequently hits capacity by 8:00 AM. Taking the RTD bus from Boulder is highly recommended.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Kelowna (YLW) or Kamloops (YKA). Extremely remote winter driving.
logistics.lodging_notes: Big White and SilverStar have incredible, massive ski-in/ski-out villages.

metadata.reputation: Canada's second-largest ski area. Massive, quiet, and family-friendly.
metadata.terrain_character: Three separate mountains. Incredible glades (Gil's) and endless rolling cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Deep, dry interior BC powder. Lighter than the coast, warmer than Banff.
metadata.logistics_notes: 45 minutes from Kamloops airport.

metadata.reputation: Famous for its massive 'Snow Ghost' trees and ski-in/ski-out village.
metadata.terrain_character: Heavy focus on intermediate cruisers and excellent, perfectly spaced tree glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Reliable, light, fluffy Okanagan powder. Frequent fog and flat light.
metadata.logistics_notes: Less than an hour from Kelowna (YLW).

metadata.reputation: Colorful, quiet village with a surprisingly aggressive backside.
metadata.terrain_character: The front side is cruisy, but the 'Dark Side' offers incredibly steep, long, un-groomed black diamond runs.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: 100% natural snow. Excellent preservation.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located just outside Vernon, BC.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Edmonton (YEG) and drive 4 hours west, or drive the Icefields Parkway from Banff (treacherous in winter).
logistics.lodging_notes: Jasper is an incredibly authentic, rustic national park town.

metadata.reputation: Far-north Alberta skiing with virtually zero crowds.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible high alpine bowls (Tres Hombres) and great, long glades.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Unbelievably dry, cold snow. One of the most northern major resorts in North America.
metadata.logistics_notes: Very remote. Jasper is much quieter and less developed than Banff.

logistics.drive_notes: Fly into Boston (BOS) and drive 2-3 hours north on I-93.
logistics.lodging_notes: Lincoln and North Conway offer massive bed bases and great mountain-town vibes.

metadata.reputation: State-owned, no-frills, fiercely rugged and steep.
metadata.terrain_character: Narrow, winding, relentlessly steep fall lines. DJ's Tramline is legendary.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Notoriously icy and windswept, but phenomenal during a heavy Nor'easter dump.
metadata.logistics_notes: Sits right in Franconia Notch, meaning weather funnels through here with extreme force.

metadata.reputation: The premier, modern mega-resort of New Hampshire.
metadata.terrain_character: Excellent terrain parks, wide cruisers, and great glades when natural snow is deep.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Massive snowmaking operation. Lower base elevation than Cannon.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely high on weekends due to direct I-93 access from Boston.
metadata.logistics_notes: Features some of the most advanced lift infrastructure in the East (8-pack bubble chairs).

metadata.reputation: Rugged, cold, and steep. Staring directly at Mount Washington.
metadata.terrain_character: Sustained steep fall lines. Excellent, challenging tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Notoriously cold and windy, but preserves natural snow beautifully.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located in Pinkham Notch, NH. Very isolated.
